metadata.title: Research the Cancer Stem Cell Debate with Claude: Hierarchy vs. Plasticity
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: NO RENDERABLE BEATS: no beat carries a shot.remotion.pattern or Manim annotation — the pipeline will render NOTHING and every slot becomes a YOU-slate. The sheet was authored without the scene contract.
B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B09: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: John Dick's 1997 AML experiment showed that only 1 in 250,000 leukemia cells could reconstitute the cancer. Irving Weissman's lab showed that almost every single melanoma cell could. The cancer stem cell model can't both be right and wrong.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: John Dick's 1997 AML experiment showed that only 1 in 250,000 leukemia cells could reconstitute the cancer. Irving Weissman's lab showed that almost every singl

beats.narration_text: Dick 1997 — CD34+CD38- cells from AML patients, transplanted into SCID mice, reconstituted leukemia. Only that tiny fraction could. This was the founding experiment of the cancer stem cell hierarchy model. Then Quintana 2008 — melanoma cells from patient tumors transplanted into NSG mice, more immunodeficient than SCID. Near 25% of melanoma cells could reconstitute cancer. Same experimental logic, completely different result. The reconciliation required identifying an experimental artifact.

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude to synthesize the debate.

props.command: claude "Research the cancer stem cell hierarchy vs. plasticity debate. Explain Dick's 1997 AML founding experiment, the Quintana 2008 melanoma challenge, and the immunodeficient mouse experimental artifact that explains the discrepancy. What is the current synthesis — can cancer cells move bidirectionally between stem and non-stem states? What are the therapeutic implications of each model?"

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Ask Claude to synthesize the debate.

props.code:
# cancer_stem_cell_brief.md  --  Claude output

## Dick 1997 (Bonnet & Dick, Nature Medicine)
- CD34+CD38- AML cells → SCID mice
- Frequency: 1/250,000 — hierarchy model founding experiment

## Quintana 2008 (Morrison lab, Nature)
- Melanoma cells → NSG mice (more immunodeficient than SCID)
- NSG mice lack NK cells; SCID retain NK cells
- Frequency: ~25% — xenograft barrier removed
- Finding: assay measured intrinsic capacity, not CSC frequency

## Current Synthesis
- Hierarchical organization exists AND
- Phenotypic plasticity between states (EMT switching)
- Cells can de-differentiate: non-stem → stem under stress

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Read the three-part synthesis brief.

beats.narration_text: Synthesis generated: The Quintana 2008 finding is not a contradiction — it is an experimental artifact revelation. SCID mice retain NK cells; NSG mice do not. NK cells kill approximately 99.9% of human cancer cells, artificially reducing reconstitution frequency. In NSG mice, the assay measures intrinsic tumorigenic capacity without immune interference. Result: both models are correct in their experimental system. Current biology: hierarchical organization exists, AND cells can de-differentiate via EMT-like transitions — moving from non-stem to stem state under stress. The plasticity model makes the therapeutic problem harder.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Synthesis generated: The Quintana 2008 finding is not a contradiction — it is an experimental artifact revelation. SCID mice retain NK cells; NSG mice do not. N

props.command: claude "Explain the therapeutic consequence of each cancer stem cell model — fixed hierarchy vs. full plasticity. If the hierarchy model is correct, what is the treatment strategy? If full plasticity, why does targeting CSCs fail?"

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Ask Claude for therapeutic consequences.

beats.narration_text: Therapeutic consequences: Hierarchy model — target only the cancer stem cells using CSC markers CD44+/CD24- and ALDH+ — a tractable minority. If you eliminate the CSC pool, the non-stem cells cannot reinitiate cancer. Plasticity model — targeting CSCs is insufficient because non-stem cells can dedifferentiate into CSCs under selection pressure — chemotherapy stress, hypoxia. Full plasticity means any surviving cell is a potential CSC under the right conditions. The implication: the hierarchy model suggests precision targeting; the plasticity model suggests you need to eliminate nearly all cells or prevent the dedifferentiation trigger.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Therapeutic consequences: Hierarchy model — target only the cancer stem cells using CSC markers CD44+/CD24- and ALDH+ — a tractable minority. If you eliminate t

beats.narration_text: The debate revealed an experimental artifact — the immunodeficient mouse model was too permissive, artificially inflating the frequency of cancer-initiating cells. The resolution is not one side was wrong but both sides were right in their specific experimental system. The biology itself — phenotypic plasticity — makes the therapeutic problem harder than the hierarchy model implies.
